old_claim.note: Grid-Scale Battery Storage is modeled as a necessary component or method for this technology in the current graph.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency edge remains from green_hydrogen to grid_scale_battery_storage; green hydrogen stays modeled through electrochemical industry and renewable energy systems instead.

source_shape.source_locator: Hydrogen overview section describing renewable electricity conversion via electrolysis and electrolysers providing grid flexibility alongside batteries.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: IRENA describes hydrogen as an energy carrier, says renewable electricity can be converted to hydrogen via electrolysis, and frames electrolysers as providing flexibility that complements alternatives such as batteries.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This rejects a hard battery-storage prerequisite because the source licenses electrolysis plus renewable electricity as the production route, while batteries appear as alternative or complementary grid-flexibility technology rather than as a component required to produce green hydrogen.

ontology_before: Green hydrogen incorrectly required grid-scale battery storage, conflating renewable-grid flexibility with the production route for electrolytic hydrogen.
ontology_after: Green hydrogen no longer depends directly on grid-scale battery storage; renewable energy systems and electrochemical production context remain as the modeled foundations.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Invariant preserved: green hydrogen remains connected to electrochemical production and renewable energy context. Changed: battery storage is not treated as a hard prerequisite for producing green hydrogen.
would_reject_if: A source showed that the scoped green_hydrogen node requires grid-scale battery storage as a component or method rather than using renewable electricity directly or through other grid arrangements.
The node were narrowed to off-grid renewable-to-hydrogen systems whose operation explicitly requires battery buffering.
A better source showed grid-scale batteries are a necessary production prerequisite rather than one possible grid-integration or flexibility technology.
short_reason: Green hydrogen is produced by electrolysis using renewable or low-carbon electricity; grid-scale batteries can support renewable integration but are not a hard production prerequisite.
